ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Vietnamese banh mi; 'sandwich food product' (FOODON_03315671) is the closest broader class. A beef-filled baguette sandwich with pickled vegetables is a culturally specific preparation not yet represented at species level in FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Sandwiches) is the closest defensible category. No specific banh mi code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for banh mi or Vietnamese sandwich as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard beef banh mi sandwich weighs approximately 200 g, comprising a 15–18 cm baguette filled with grilled beef slices, pickled daikon and carrot, cucumber, fresh coriander, chilli, and condiments.

nutrient_source.title: Beef Banh Mi — Singapore Health Promotion Board Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Foodo database entry for Beef Banh Mi (cr_id F05020004). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central beef sandwich composite (USDA SR Legacy #21095) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this item; this is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for beef banh mi. GI estimated at 68 (medium) based on the GI of white wheat baguette/French bread (GI ~70, University of Sydney GI Database) as the dominant carbohydrate vehicle, adjusted slightly downward to 68 to account for the protein and fat content of the beef and condiment filling, which moderates the glycaemic response. Glycaemic load per 200 g serving = (68 × 20 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 2) / 100 = 27.2.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content at 431 mg per 100 g, primarily from fish sauce, soy sauce, and pickled vegetables
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid beef banh mi due to its sodium content of 431 mg/100 g (862 mg per standard 200 g serving), which represents approximately 37% of the 2,300 mg daily sodium limit; requesting reduced condiments and substituting pickled vegetables with fresh cucumber and coriander can meaningfully lower sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined white baguette with medium-high GI (~68) and low dietary fibre (1.2 g/100 g) promotes rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should consume beef banh mi cautiously; the white baguette contributes a medium-high GI of approximately 68 and a glycaemic load of ~27 per serving, and the low fibre content (1.2 g/100 g) provides insufficient buffering — choosing a wholemeal baguette or reducing the bread portion by half can substantially lower the glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (431 mg/100 g) and moderate saturated fat from grilled beef raise cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the high sodium load (431 mg/100 g) and moderate saturated fat from beef (~2.1 g/100 g estimated) are the primary concerns; limiting portion size to 100–120 g, requesting reduced condiments, and choosing leaner beef cuts or substituting with grilled chicken can reduce both sodium and saturated fat exposure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat provides arachidonic acid amplifying inflammatory pathways; high sodium worsens tissue swelling; chilli (nightshade) may trigger joint sensitivity
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or minimise beef banh mi as the red meat contributes arachidonic acid (omega-6) that amplifies inflammatory pathways, the 431 mg/100 g sodium load worsens RA-related tissue swelling, and chilli as a nightshade vegetable may trigger solanine-related joint sensitivity in susceptible individuals — substituting beef with grilled fish or chicken and requesting fresh vegetables instead of pickled ones is advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef banh mi (Vietnamese: Bánh mì bò) is a Vietnamese sandwich that fuses French colonial baguette tradition with Southeast Asian flavours. It consists of a crispy, light French baguette filled with slices of grilled or marinated beef, pickled daikon radish and carrot, fresh cucumber, coriander (cilantro), sliced chilli, and condiments such as mayonnaise, pâté, soy sauce, and fish sauce. The result is a sandwich with contrasting textures — a crunchy exterior, tender beef, and crunchy pickled vegetables — and a complex flavour profile combining savoury, tangy, spicy, and herbaceous notes. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 190 kcal, 13.1 g protein, 6.43 g fat, and 20 g carbohydrate, with a notable sodium content of 431 mg primarily from condiments and pickled vegetables. The dietary fibre content is low at 1.2 g/100 g. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 68 (medium) due to the refined white baguette, making it a moderate-to-high glycaemic food. Clinically, it is unsuitable for individuals with hypertension due to high sodium, requires caution for those with type 2 diabetes due to the refined carbohydrate base and medium-high GI, and poses moderate cardiovascular risk from combined sodium and saturated fat. It is widely consumed as a breakfast or lunch item across Vietnam and in Vietnamese communities in Southeast Asia, including Singapore and Malaysia.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a beef banh mi?
Is banh mi high in sodium?
What is in a beef banh mi?
Is banh mi suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Foodo database (cr_id F05020004) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy beef sandwich composite as HPB does not separately report this value. GI is an analogous estimate based on white baguette GI from the University of Sydney GI Database. All other values are from the HPB primary source.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (2.1 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for beef sandwich composite (#21095) as HPB Foodo does not separately report saturated fat for this item. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as these values are not reported in the HPB Foodo database for this item and no sufficiently close approved-source composite was available to estimate them reliably. 3. GI of 68 is an analogous estimate based on white wheat baguette/French bread (GI ~70, University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement for beef banh mi was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of April 2026. 4. Cultural tags corrected: primary cuisine is Vietnamese (not 'western' as in input); ethnic tags updated accordingly. 5. Dietary tags corrected: 'vegetarian' and 'plant_based' removed as this dish contains beef; 'high_protein' retained as protein is 13.1 g/100 g. 6. Input glycemic_index of 0 and glycemic_load of 0.0 were unsupported and have been replaced with evidence-based estimates. 7. Halal status retained as plausible for standard preparation without pork-derived pâté; consumers should verify pâté ingredients at point of purchase.
